results.editorial:
KS Care Centre (凯心安老院) sits at No. 28, Jalan Ikhlas 5 in Bandar Sungai Long, a residential township on the Kajang–Cheras fringe of Selangor. The Chinese name — meaning roughly "benevolent heart elder home" — signals its primary audience: Mandarin-speaking families looking for a home-like environment rather than a clinical institution. The centre operates 365 days a year with stated 24-hour caregiving coverage, positioning itself as a full residential option rather than a day-centre. A second branch exists in Shah Alam, suggesting modest chain ambitions.

The service menu spans six named categories: Senior Citizen Care, Stroke and Cancer Care, Dementia Care, Handicapped Care, Retirement Resort, and Home Nursing. Staff are described as having up to 25 years of hospital experience, though exact nurse-to-resident ratios and clinical qualifications are not publicly disclosed. Pricing found online lists four-bed rooms at RM 2,000–2,300/month, three-bed at RM 2,500, and two-bed at RM 2,700, with a first-three-months promotional rate from RM 1,700 — placing it in the budget-to-mid range for Kajang. Hours of 8:30am to 10pm suggest visiting is structured rather than open-ended.

Before committing, families should ask specifically what the dementia care offering entails — whether it involves a dedicated wing or just higher supervision. It is also worth clarifying staffing levels overnight, given the 8:30am–10pm posted hours versus the claimed 24-hour care. The Shah Alam branch may have different standards to the Kajang original, so visiting the specific site your relative would enter is essential. Bed count, licence number, and physiotherapy availability are all gaps to close during enquiry.

facts.care_types: Nursing home, Dementia care, Stroke and cancer care, Handicapped care, Home nursing
facts.red_flags: Operating hours posted as 8:30am–10pm conflicts with claim of 24-hour care; no published staffing ratios; bed count unknown

results.skip_reason: No web presence, social media, or third-party directory entry found for this facility. Only Google Maps pin in Taman Bukit Indah (Kajang/Selangor border area). Rating of 5.0 from a single review provides no useful signal. Insufficient verified information to write a responsible editorial.

facts.red_flags: No verifiable online presence; single Google review only; unable to confirm operational status

results.editorial:
Avicenna Nursing Centre occupies a shophouse unit at No. 64-A, Jalan Renang 13/26 in Tadisma Business Park, Shah Alam's Seksyen 13 — a light-industrial and commercial zone about 2 km from Avisena Specialist Hospital (note the different spelling; the two are unrelated). The name references Ibn Sina, the medieval Islamic physician, which alongside the location in predominantly Malay Shah Alam may hint at a Muslim-oriented operation, though this is unconfirmed. The centre is listed simply as a nursing home with a single mobile contact number and no public website or Facebook page found.

Publicly available information about Avicenna Nursing Centre is extremely limited. No service menu, bed count, staffing details, or pricing have been published online. The shophouse unit format in a business park typically suggests a smaller facility — likely under 30 beds — operating on a practical rather than resort model. Proximity to Avisena Specialist Hospital and the broader Shah Alam Hospital catchment may be an operational advantage for residents needing frequent specialist appointments.

Families should treat this as a facility requiring a full in-person visit before any decision. Key questions include: JKM registration and licence number, total bed capacity, nurse coverage at night, whether the unit has been purpose-fitted or is a basic conversion, and what clinical capabilities (wound care, catheter management, NGT feeding) are on offer. The shophouse setting in an industrial park also warrants a look at the physical environment — ventilation, outdoor access, and amenities for residents who are ambulant.

facts.red_flags: No website or social media; shophouse unit in industrial park; extremely thin online footprint; requires in-person verification

results.skip_reason: Listed as a nursing agency in Kawasan 17 (Klang area). No website, Facebook page, or directory listing found under this name. The 'Kawasan 17' designation suggests Klang's industrial zone. With no published information about services, staff, or operations, there is insufficient verified material to write a responsible editorial.

facts.red_flags: No verifiable online presence; listed as nursing agency rather than residential home; unable to confirm services or operational status

results.skip_reason: Facility is listed in Taman Serdang Raya (Seri Kembangan area) but no website, Facebook page, or substantive directory entry found. The name 'Penyayang' (caring/compassionate) is common and several unrelated facilities share similar names. Single Google review provides no useful signal. Insufficient verified information to write a responsible editorial.

facts.red_flags: No verifiable online presence; common name — risk of confusion with other Penyayang-named centres; unable to confirm operational status

results.editorial:
Pusat Jagaan Warga Emas Kangiten (PJWEK) operates from 29, Jalan SP1 in Taman Selayang Jaya, Batu Caves — a residential estate north of Kuala Lumpur that sits within easy reach of Hospital Selayang, one of the country's larger public tertiary centres. The facility markets itself explicitly as both a nursing home and a physiotherapy centre, which is a meaningful distinction: rather than treating physio as a bolt-on, it appears to be a core service pillar. The team description references doctors, nurses, and physiotherapists on staff, though headcounts and shift arrangements are not publicly stated.

Kangiten's clinical capability list is more detailed than most facilities at this level. Documented nursing procedures include wound dressing, bed-sore management, catheter and tracheostomy care, NGT insertion, insulin administration, and medication monitoring — a range that suggests the centre is equipped to accept residents with moderate-to-high dependency. On the rehabilitation side, the facility's physiotherapists reportedly cover home visits across 14 service zones, and treat conditions including stroke, Parkinson's, arthritis, cerebral palsy, joint replacements, and spinal cord injuries. Day care and short-term stay options appear to exist alongside long-term residential places.

Pricing and total bed count are not published, so families should request these directly. The home-visit physiotherapy arm may operate somewhat independently of the residential unit — worth clarifying whether residents automatically access in-house physio or whether this is separately charged. Confirming the JKM licence number and the on-site medical cover arrangement (particularly overnight) are standard due-diligence steps before admission. The Batu Caves location has good road access but limited public transport, which matters for families visiting by bus or LRT.

facts.care_types: Nursing home, Physiotherapy, Day care, Short-term care
facts.red_flags: Pricing and bed count not published; physio home-visit arm may be distinct from residential care; overnight medical cover needs confirming

results.editorial:
Pusat Jagaan Rumah Orang Tua Wesley is the residential care arm of the Wesley Outreach Society, which operates under Wesley Methodist Church. The home sits at No. 8, Jalan Meru, off Lorong Jugra, Jalan Klang Lama — an older residential road in the Kuala Lumpur–Selangor border corridor. Although the Google Maps record places it in Selangor, the address is in the Jalan Klang Lama corridor, and the administrator contact uses a 03- Kuala Lumpur prefix. The facility is a mission-driven, non-profit home rather than a commercial operator, which typically means tighter budgets but a strong ethos of pastoral care.

Admission criteria are clearly stated: applicants must be 60 or older, have no family able to care for them, and be both mobile and healthy at the time of entry. Required documentation includes a medical report with X-ray and blood test results. This is notably selective — the home is designed for independent-ish seniors who need companionship and basic support, not high-dependency nursing care. A supervisor (Mr Peter Lim) manages enquiries, and application forms are available at the church office or the home itself. Bed count and detailed services have not been publicly disclosed.

For families with a Methodist or broader Christian background seeking a modest, community-rooted option, this home is worth a direct enquiry. However, the mobile-and-healthy admission standard means it is unsuitable for residents with significant cognitive impairment, complex nursing needs, or limited mobility. Families should ask whether residents who decline in health can remain, or whether they would need to transfer to a higher-care facility — that transition plan matters a great deal for long-term peace of mind. Pricing is not published and likely involves a subsidy structure given the church affiliation.

facts.red_flags: Accepts only mobile and healthy seniors — not suitable for nursing-dependent residents; unclear what happens if health deteriorates; bed count and pricing not public

results.editorial:
Mintygreen Care Centre @ USJ21 is one branch in Mintygreen's growing Klang Valley network, operating from 1, Jalan USJ 21/10 in UEP Subang Jaya — a mature township with excellent road access and proximity to Subang Jaya Medical Centre (SJMC) and several public clinics. Mintygreen Healthcare Group, established in 2017, is known as an early mover in integrating Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM) into its nursing home model, and this positioning runs across all branches. The USJ21 unit is a smaller, neighbourhood-scale branch rather than a flagship; the main Kajang–Balakong site serves as the group's headquarters.

Across the Mintygreen network, the group's documented capabilities include 24-hour nursing care, post-surgery recovery, regular doctor visits, physiotherapy, rehabilitation, psychological counselling, TCM (acupuncture and Tui Na), dietetic services, day care, and home care through its '24 Angels' arm. Whether all of these services are available at the USJ21 branch specifically — or concentrated at larger sites — is not publicly stated and should be confirmed directly. The contact person listed for USJ21 is Ms. Xiao Tong at +60 11-5587 9788, distinct from the group's main line, which suggests branch-level management.

For families in Subang Jaya or USJ who want a local option without travelling to Kajang, this branch is a reasonable first call — but it warrants a site visit to assess the physical space, bed count, and what share of Mintygreen's full capability is actually on-site. Pricing is not published for this branch; the group's rates at other locations tend toward the mid-market range for Klang Valley. Ask specifically about doctor visit frequency, physio session costs, and whether TCM is integrated or a pay-per-session extra.

facts.red_flags: Branch-level detail sparse — unclear which group services are available on-site vs at HQ; bed count and pricing not published for this location

results.editorial:
Unique Elderly Care Centre is based at No. 22, Jalan 17/1 in Seksyen 17, Petaling Jaya — a well-connected residential address close to both the LDP and Federal Highway, with Assunta Hospital and Pantai Medical Centre within 10–15 minutes. The centre operates 24 hours daily and explicitly lists a broad clinical capability set on its website, making it one of the more transparent facilities in the Petaling Jaya market. It serves residents requiring moderate to high nursing dependency, including those with dementia.

Published capabilities include wound management, tracheostomy care, catheter management, colostomy care, and palliative care — suggesting the centre can support end-of-life residents. Clinical equipment listed includes hospital beds, ripple and air mattresses, oxygen machines, and suction machines. In-house nurses are on staff, with physiotherapy available and a doctor-on-call arrangement. The facility also offers transportation for scheduled hospital visits, 24-hour CCTV monitoring, and day care in addition to long-term residential placements. Meals are described as local Chinese cuisine with five servings daily; amenities include Astro, WiFi, and newspapers.

Pricing and exact bed count are not published on the centre's website, so families should enquire directly for current rates. The Chinese food emphasis suggests the centre's primary demographic is Chinese-speaking residents; families from other backgrounds should ask about meal flexibility. It is worth confirming the doctor-on-call arrangement — whether this is a retained GP or an on-demand service — and what the caregiver-to-resident ratio looks like during night hours. The JKM licence number should be requested and cross-checked as a standard step.

facts.care_types: Nursing home, Dementia care, Palliative care, Day care
facts.languages: Mandarin (inferred from Chinese food emphasis), English
facts.red_flags: Pricing and bed count not published; doctor-on-call model needs clarification; Chinese-centric meal offering may not suit all residents

results.editorial:
Siri Jayanti Metta Care Centre — locally known as Metta Home — is a welfare-oriented old folks home run by Pertubuhan Kebajikan Siri Jayanti (Siri Jayanti Welfare Organisation), a Buddhist charity founded in 1991. The home is located at 2 Jalan Perusahaan Satu, in the Kawasan Perusahaan PKNS industrial estate near Setapak, Kuala Lumpur — though some records list it under a Selangor postcode given the estate's administrative boundary. The facility relocated from Ampang to its current site in 2004 and has around 20 beds, based on the organisation's own published figures. It operates entirely on public donations, with no commercial fee model.

Metta Home's primary mission is to shelter neglected and low-income elderly who have no family support. The organisation explicitly states it accepts residents regardless of race, creed, or religion, despite its Buddhist character — a practical inclusivity uncommon in faith-based homes. The SJWO runs broader welfare programmes (food aid, education support, charity events) that give the home an active volunteer and donor network, which translates into periodic external support — charity visits, food donations, and fundraising drives that supplement day-to-day operations. The care model is basic residential support: shelter, meals, and companionship rather than clinical nursing.

Families should understand that Metta Home is a charity home, not a licensed nursing facility in the clinical sense. With roughly 20 residents and donation-dependent funding, staffing and facilities are modest. It is best suited for seniors who are relatively mobile and need supported living rather than complex nursing care. Families considering this home should visit in person, ask about the current resident-to-caregiver ratio, and understand what happens if a resident's health declines and clinical nursing becomes necessary. Admission is likely means-tested and subject to availability — contact the SJWO sub-committee directly to enquire.

facts.red_flags: Donation-funded only — budget constraints may affect staffing and facilities; approximately 20 beds; not a clinical nursing facility; industrial estate location; no formal nursing capability confirmed
